section.title: Thanksgiving for Philemon's Love
section.sectionRationale: Before Paul asks anything, he gives thanks. He praises Philemon's faith and love, names his gospel partnership, and celebrates how he has refreshed the hearts of the saints. This thanksgiving is not flattery; it is the very ground on which the coming appeal will stand.
section.thesis: Paul thanks God for a faith that works itself out in love, and he holds up the very virtue, a heart that refreshes others, that he will soon ask Philemon to extend to Onesimus.
section.frameNote: One movement: Paul's constant thanks, his report of Philemon's faith and love, his prayer for an effective partnership, and his joy that Philemon refreshes the saints.

verses.text: I always thank my God, remembering you in my prayers,
because I hear about your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love for all the saints.
I pray that your {{koinonia|partnership}} in the faith may become effective as you fully acknowledge every good thing that is ours in Christ.
I take great joy and encouragement in your love, because you, brother, have {{anapauo|refreshed}} the {{splagchna|hearts}} of the saints.

koinonia.gloss: Partnership, fellowship, a sharing in common. It describes the deep mutual life believers share in Christ, more than friendship, a joint stake in the gospel and in one another. Paul prays this partnership will become effective, and he will later ask Philemon, on the basis of their partnership, to receive Onesimus as he would receive Paul himself.

anapauo.gloss: To refresh, give rest, relieve, or revive. Philemon has been refreshing the weary hearts of the saints, a track record of practical love. Paul lifts up this very quality, and near the end of the letter he will ask Philemon to refresh his heart too by welcoming Onesimus.

splagchna.gloss: Literally the inward parts, the bowels, used figuratively for the seat of deep affection and compassion, the heart. Philemon has refreshed the splagchna of the saints. The word threads through the letter: Onesimus is Paul's own heart, and Paul asks Philemon to refresh his heart by receiving him.

basic.context: Before Paul raises the hard matter of Onesimus, he tells Philemon how thankful he is for him. He has heard reports of Philemon's real faith in Jesus and his love for God's people, and he prays that this love would keep bearing fruit. He especially notes that Philemon has a way of refreshing tired believers. This is genuine praise, and it is also the very kindness Paul is about to ask him to show to a returning slave.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not empty flattery. Paul is thankful for real things and says so, and by naming Philemon's love and his habit of refreshing others, he gently reminds him of the kind of man he already is. The passage shows us how to encourage people truthfully and to build any hard request on real gratitude.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul prays that Philemon's koinonia, his partnership or sharing in the faith, would become effective. He rejoices that Philemon has refreshed, anapauo, the hearts of the saints. That word for hearts is splagchna, the deep inward affections. Each of these words comes back later: the partnership, the refreshing, and the heart all reappear when Paul makes his plea for Onesimus.
basic.typology: Philemon's love that refreshes the weary is a small echo of Christ, who says to the heavy laden, Come to Me and I will give you rest. The love that flows from faith and revives others reflects the Lord who is the true refresher of tired souls.
basic.christ: The good that Philemon does flows from every good thing that is ours in Christ. His faith is in the Lord Jesus, and his love for the saints is the fruit of union with Christ. Christ is both the source of Philemon's love and the pattern of the refreshing rest that love brings.
basic.god: Paul thanks God, not Philemon, for Philemon's goodness, because God is the giver of the faith and love that Philemon shows. God is the One who works real change in people and delights to be thanked for the fruit He produces in His saints.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 5:6 speaks of faith working through love, exactly what Paul sees in Philemon. Matthew 11:28 has Jesus offering rest to the weary, the refreshing Philemon imitates. First Thessalonians 1:3 shows Paul again giving thanks for faith and love in action.
basic.oneStory: Real faith always works itself out in love that serves others. Philemon's love, praised here, is the seedbed for the reconciliation Paul will ask for, and it shows the shape of the whole Christian life: faith in Christ overflowing into costly love for His people.

context.literary: The thanksgiving section that in Paul's letters typically follows the greeting and often previews the letter's themes. Here it does so masterfully: faith, love, partnership, and the refreshing of hearts are all named now and all return in the appeal. Paul praises the very virtues he is about to call on.
context.historical: Paul writes on the strength of reports he has heard, likely through Epaphras and others, about Philemon's standing in the Colossian church. Philemon is evidently known for hospitality and practical care that has lifted the spirits of fellow believers, a reputation Paul honors and then leans upon.
context.canonical: The passage joins the New Testament's steady witness that saving faith is never barren but always issues in love. It also models Christian encouragement, giving God thanks for genuine grace at work in others before ever asking anything of them.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To thank God sincerely for Philemon's faith and love, to remind Philemon of the generous man he already is, and to lay a foundation of gratitude and shared partnership on which the appeal for Onesimus can rest.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Paul's thanksgiving and Philemon's character; the enduring pattern it prescribes is a faith that works through love, and encouragement that is truthful and God-directed rather than manipulative flattery.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by praying that Philemon's partnership in the faith may become effective?

views.label: Effective in good deeds and love
views.case: Paul prays that Philemon's shared life in the gospel would keep bearing practical fruit, coming to full effect in acts of love, of which welcoming Onesimus would be one.

views.label: Effective in deepening knowledge of Christ
views.case: The verse ties effectiveness to fully acknowledging every good thing that is ours in Christ, so the partnership grows as Philemon grasps more of the riches he has in the gospel.

debates.bearing: Both readings meet in the same aim: a partnership in the gospel that is alive and working, so that grasping what we have in Christ produces love that acts, exactly what Paul will ask of Philemon.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's thanksgiving quietly loads the words he will need later. He prays that Philemon's koinonia, his partnership in the faith, would become effective; on the basis of that same partnership he will later say, receive him as you would receive me. He rejoices that Philemon has refreshed, anapauo, the hearts of the saints, and near the close he pleads, refresh my heart in Christ. And the word for hearts is splagchna, the deep inward affections; Onesimus, Paul will say, is his own splagchna. The thanksgiving is planting seeds it will harvest.

typology.type: Philemon refreshing the weary hearts of the saints
typology.fulfillment: A reflection of Christ, who gives rest to all who are weary and heavy laden, Matthew 11:28; the love that revives others mirrors the Lord who is the true refresher of souls.

study.christ: Everything Paul praises flows from Christ. Philemon's faith is faith in the Lord Jesus, his love is the fruit of union with Him, and the partnership Paul prays for grows by acknowledging every good thing that is ours in Christ. Christ is the source of the love that refreshes others and the pattern of the rest it brings. The costly welcome Paul will ask for is only possible because such love has its wellspring in Jesus.
study.god: Paul directs his thanks to God, not to Philemon, because God is the giver of the faith and love that Philemon displays. God works real transformation in His people, producing faith that acts and love that serves, and He delights when that fruit is noticed and He is thanked for it. The good in Philemon is finally a testimony to the God who is at work in him.

crossReferences.note: Faith expressing itself through love, precisely what Paul recognizes and gives thanks for in Philemon.
Jesus gives rest to the weary, the refreshing that Philemon's love imitates among the saints.
Paul again thanks God for faith and love shown in action, his pattern of God-directed encouragement.
Faith without works is dead; the living faith Paul praises here is faith that produces love.

study.oneStory: Saving faith is never barren; it always works itself out in love that serves. Philemon's love, honored here, is the soil out of which the reconciliation Paul seeks will grow. This little thanksgiving shows the shape of the whole Christian life, faith in Christ overflowing into costly love for His people, and it prepares the way for the hardest act of love the letter will ask.

sermon.title: Terms of Endearment
sermon.why: The opening message of Begg's series A Study in Philemon covers Philemon 1:1-7, including this thanksgiving, and draws out how Philemon's faith and love and his refreshing of the saints set the stage for Paul's appeal. A faithful, expository treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does Paul thank God before making any request of Philemon?

options.text: He is genuinely grateful for Philemon's faith and love, and this real gratitude grounds the appeal to come.
options.note: Paul builds on true thanksgiving, not flattery, honoring the man Philemon already is.

options.text: It is just a polite formula he uses in every letter.
options.note: Paul does open with thanksgiving often, but here it is specific and genuine, previewing the letter's themes.

options.text: He is buttering Philemon up to manipulate him.
options.note: Paul's praise is sincere and God-directed, not manipulative flattery.

options.text: He had nothing important to say yet.
options.note: The thanksgiving is deliberate and lays the groundwork for the whole appeal.

quiz.q: What two qualities does Paul specifically thank God for in Philemon?

options.text: His faith in the Lord Jesus and his love for all the saints.
options.note: Faith and love, the faith that works itself out in love, are the heart of Paul's thanksgiving.

options.text: His wealth and his generous hospitality.
options.note: Philemon was hospitable, but Paul names faith and love as the deeper qualities.

options.text: His knowledge of Scripture and his teaching gift.
options.note: These are not what Paul highlights here; he praises faith and love.
Paul's thanks center on Philemon's faith and love, not these.

quiz.q: What has Philemon done that especially brings Paul joy?

options.text: He has refreshed the hearts of the saints through his love.
options.note: Paul lifts up this very quality, and will later ask Philemon to refresh his heart by receiving Onesimus.

options.text: He has shown generous hospitality to God's people.
options.note: Philemon was hospitable, yet the text specifically praises how he refreshed the saints' hearts.

options.text: He has given large sums of money to Paul's ministry.
options.note: The text speaks of refreshing hearts, not financial giving.

options.text: He has won many arguments for the faith.
options.note: Paul commends love that refreshes, not skill in debate.

quiz.q: How does this thanksgiving prepare for the appeal that follows?

options.text: The partnership, the refreshing, and the heart that Paul praises here all return when he pleads for Onesimus.
options.note: Paul plants the very words, koinonia, anapauo, and splagchna, that he will later harvest in his appeal.

options.text: It warms Philemon's heart and disposes him kindly before the request.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but more precisely, the very words and themes here return in the appeal.

options.text: It has no connection to the rest of the letter.
options.note: In fact the thanksgiving carefully previews the letter's key themes and words.

options.text: It warns Philemon of coming judgment.
options.note: The tone is warm thanksgiving, not warning.

questions: Paul noticed and named the good he saw in Philemon before asking anything of him. Who in your life could you thank God for out loud this week, and tell them why?
Philemon was known for refreshing the weary hearts of the saints. Whose heart could you refresh right now, and what would that practically look like?
Paul's thanks went to God for the good in Philemon. When you admire something in another believer, do you trace it back to God's work in them?
Real faith shows itself in love. Where is your faith in Christ currently working itself out in costly love for others, and where has it grown quiet?
The love praised here became the seedbed for a hard act of reconciliation. How might the ordinary kindness you practice now be preparing you for a costly obedience later?

translationNotes.note: Partnership in the faith renders koinonia, a shared life and stake in the gospel; the phrase can point both to fellowship among believers and to a shared grasp of all that is ours in Christ.
Hearts renders splagchna, literally the inward parts, used for the deep seat of affection; the same word recurs in verses 12 and 20, tying the thanksgiving to the appeal.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philemon 1:4-7. No em dashes appear in this movement's text.
